senses.definition: Secret or private meetings, especially between lovers.
examples: Rumours spread about his late-night assignations with a colleague.
The novel follows the lovers through a series of secret assignations.
They arranged their assignations by encrypted message to avoid attention.

senses.definition: Paper money issued in France during the French Revolution, based on the value of confiscated church lands.
examples: The government printed more assignats to pay its debts.
Hyperinflation soon made the assignats almost worthless.
Historians still debate whether issuing assignats was necessary.

senses.definition: Given someone a task, duty, or piece of work to do.
examples: The manager assigned me to lead the new project.
Students were assigned three chapters to read before class.
Each volunteer was assigned a specific role for the event.

senses.definition: Given or set for a particular person, purpose, or situation.
examples: I finished all my assigned tasks early.
Each team will work in its assigned area.
Please submit the assigned documents by Friday.

senses.definition: A person to whom a right, property, or responsibility is officially given.
examples: The contract clearly identifies the assignee of the intellectual property rights.
Any assignee must agree to the original terms of the lease.
The creditor notified the assignee about the transfer of the claim.

senses.definition: People to whom rights, property, or responsibilities are officially given.
examples: The agreement binds the parties and their successors and assignees.
The landlord must approve any proposed assignees of the lease.
Creditors contacted all assignees before finalizing the settlement.

senses.definition: The position, role, or period of serving as an assignee.
examples: He accepted the assigneeship of the estate during the bankruptcy proceedings.
Her assigneeship lasted until all creditors were paid.
The court carefully defined the duties connected to the assigneeship.

senses.definition: A person or organization that gives someone a task, duty, or something such as a right or property.
examples: The assigner must explain the purpose of each task clearly.
The assigner of the copyright remains the original author.
Conflicts can occur if the assigner does not communicate expectations.

senses.definition: People or organizations that give tasks, duties, or rights to others.
examples: Project assigners should consider each employee’s strengths.
The law protects both assigners and assignees in the contract.
Different assigners may use different criteria for distributing work.

senses.definition: Giving someone a particular task, duty, place, or thing.
examples: The teacher is assigning partners for the group project.
Managers are responsible for assigning work fairly.
The software helps with assigning priorities to tasks.

senses.definition: A task or piece of work that someone is given to do.
examples: I have a math assignment due tomorrow.
The manager gave me a new assignment this week.
She stayed up late to finish her assignment.

senses.definition: A particular job, position, or place someone is sent to for a period of time.
examples: Her first overseas assignment was in Singapore.
He is on a two-year assignment in the London office.
The journalist accepted a dangerous assignment in the conflict zone.

senses.definition: The act of officially giving a right, claim, or property to another person.
examples: The assignment of rights must be in writing to be valid.
They discussed the assignment of the lease to a new tenant.
Assignment of intellectual property is covered in the contract.

senses.definition: Tasks or pieces of work that people are given to do.
examples: Students often have several assignments to complete each week.
I try to start my assignments early to avoid stress.
Her job involves managing multiple assignments at once.

senses.definition: Jobs, positions, or places people are sent to for a period of time.
examples: Their international assignments helped them build global experience.
She enjoyed her previous assignments in Europe.
Officers rotated between different field assignments.

senses.definition: A person or organization that transfers a right, claim, or property to another.
examples: The assignor must have legal ownership of the property.
The contract identifies the assignor and the assignee.
Any promises made by the assignor remain binding after the transfer.

senses.definition: People or organizations that transfer rights, claims, or property to others.
examples: The agreement protects both assignors and assignees.
Assignors must disclose any existing claims on the property.
Multiple assignors signed the transfer documents.

senses.definition: Gives someone a particular task, duty, place, or thing.
examples: The teacher assigns homework every Friday.
The system automatically assigns each ticket to a support agent.
The coach assigns positions based on players’ strengths.

senses.definition: People to whom rights, property, or responsibilities are legally given, often mentioned in formal documents.
examples: The agreement binds the parties and their heirs and assigns.
This policy applies to the company and its assigns.
The landlord, his successors, and assigns retain certain rights.

senses.definition: A local British name for the European storm petrel, a small sea bird.
examples: The fisherman pointed out an assilag flying close to the waves.
Locals sometimes call the storm petrel an assilag.
An assilag circled the boat in the evening light.

senses.definition: The qualities of being easy to absorb, understand, or integrate.
examples: Researchers compared the assimilabilities of different teaching materials.
Food technologists study the assimilabilities of various nutrients.
The two cultures have different assimilabilities within the host society.

senses.definition: The degree to which something can be absorbed, understood, or integrated into a system or group.
examples: The assimilability of new vocabulary depends on how often learners meet it.
Policy makers debate the assimilability of different migrant groups.
The assimilability of this nutrient makes it useful in supplements.

senses.definition: Able to be absorbed, understood, or integrated into something larger.
examples: The lecture notes are written in a very assimilable style.
Proteins in this form are more easily assimilable by the body.
The government considered some groups more assimilable than others.

senses.definition: In a way that can be easily absorbed, understood, or integrated.
examples: The theory is not assimilably presented for beginners.
Nutrients in this formula are more assimilably delivered.
The key points were assimilably summarized at the end.

senses.definition: To fully take in and understand new information or ideas.
examples: It takes time to assimilate so much new information.
Students need space to assimilate what they have learned.
The training helped staff assimilate the new procedures.

senses.definition: To become part of a new culture or group, or make someone become part of it.
examples: Immigrants often find it hard to assimilate into a new society.
The policy aims to assimilate minority groups into the mainstream culture.
Children tend to assimilate more quickly than adults.

senses.definition: When a body or organism takes in and uses food or nutrients.
examples: The body assimilates nutrients from the food we eat.
Calcium is more easily assimilated in this form.
Patients with this condition cannot assimilate fats properly.

senses.definition: Fully taken in and understood new information or ideas.
examples: I have not yet assimilated all the details of the report.
Once they assimilated the feedback, they improved their performance.
The students quickly assimilated the main concepts.

senses.definition: Became part of a new culture or group, or made someone become part of it.
examples: Many families quickly assimilated into the local community.
The dominant culture assimilated smaller ethnic groups.
He felt he had fully assimilated into the team by the end of the year.

senses.definition: Fully integrated or absorbed into a larger group, system, or structure.
examples: They feel like fully assimilated members of society.
Assimilated immigrants often act as bridges between cultures.
The new policies are now assimilated parts of the organization.

senses.definition: Fully takes in and understands new information or ideas.
examples: The brain assimilates new information during sleep.
She quickly assimilates complex theories.
The course helps learners assimilate what they read and hear.

senses.definition: Becomes part of a new culture or group, or makes someone become part of it.
examples: The community slowly assimilates newcomers.
He assimilates into new environments with ease.
Society often expects minorities to assimilate quickly.

senses.definition: Fully taking in and understanding new information or ideas.
examples: She is still assimilating the results of the study.
Assimilating so many new concepts at once can be tiring.
They spent time assimilating the changes in the law.

senses.definition: Becoming part of a new culture or group, or making someone become part of it.
examples: The family is still assimilating into the local community.
The program focuses on assimilating refugees into society.
Assimilating into a new workplace can take several months.

senses.definition: The process of fully understanding and using new information or ideas.
examples: Good teaching allows time for the assimilation of new knowledge.
The course encourages active assimilation of complex theories.
Assimilation of feedback is essential for improvement.

senses.definition: The process by which a person or group becomes part of another culture or society.
examples: The government promotes the assimilation of immigrants into mainstream society.
Complete assimilation can take several generations.
Some communities resist cultural assimilation to preserve their traditions.

senses.definition: The process by which the body takes in and uses food or nutrients.
examples: Assimilation of nutrients is slower in older adults.
Diet plays a major role in the assimilation of vitamins.
The doctor explained how digestion and assimilation work together.

senses.definition: A change in a sound in speech so that it becomes more like a nearby sound.
examples: In fast speech, assimilation often affects consonant sounds.
Linguists study assimilation in connected speech.
The /n/ in 'input' shows assimilation to the following /p/ sound.

senses.definition: The belief or policy that minority groups should become similar to the dominant culture.
examples: Assimilationism has shaped many immigration policies.
Critics argue that assimilationism ignores cultural diversity.
The debate between multiculturalism and assimilationism continues.

senses.definition: Different forms or expressions of the belief or policy that minorities should become similar to the dominant culture.
examples: Various assimilationisms emerged in response to large-scale migration.
The article compares European assimilationisms in the 20th century.
Modern assimilationisms are often challenged by human rights groups.

senses.definition: A person who supports or promotes the idea that minority groups should become similar to the dominant culture.
examples: The politician was described as an assimilationist by his critics.
Assimilationists argue that shared values create social unity.
She is no assimilationist; she believes in protecting minority traditions.

senses.definition: Supporting the idea that minority groups should become similar to the dominant culture.
examples: The government introduced assimilationist policies in the 1960s.
His assimilationist views clash with modern ideas of diversity.
Assimilationist models of education are now widely questioned.

senses.definition: People who support or promote the idea that minority groups should become similar to the dominant culture.
examples: Assimilationists and multiculturalists often clash in policy debates.
Some assimilationists argue that separate communities weaken social unity.
Critics accuse assimilationists of ignoring minority rights.

senses.definition: Processes of fully understanding and using new information or ideas.
examples: Different students show different rates of assimilations of new material.
The workshop encourages quick assimilations of key concepts.
Repeated practice leads to deeper assimilations of grammar rules.

senses.definition: Processes by which people or groups become part of another culture or society.
examples: The book compares different immigrant assimilations over time.
Forced assimilations often lead to resentment.
Voluntary assimilations can still involve a loss of traditions.

senses.definition: Relating to or showing the ability to absorb and integrate new information, ideas, or elements.
examples: Children have strong assimilative powers when learning languages.
The organization shows an assimilative culture that welcomes change.
His mind is highly assimilative and open to new theories.

senses.definition: In a way that absorbs and integrates new information, ideas, or elements.
examples: The brain works assimilatively when relating new facts to old ones.
Cultures interact assimilatively as they exchange customs.
She thinks assimilatively, connecting concepts from different fields.

senses.definition: The quality of being able to absorb and integrate new information, ideas, or elements.
examples: Children's high assimilativeness helps them acquire language quickly.
The assimilativeness of the institution makes it open to reform.
Individual differences in assimilativeness affect learning styles.

senses.definition: A person, group, or thing that absorbs and integrates new people, ideas, or elements.
examples: The dominant culture often acts as an assimilator of minority customs.
He is a quick assimilator of new information.
The company became a powerful assimilator of smaller firms.

senses.definition: People, groups, or things that absorb and integrate new people, ideas, or elements.
examples: Teachers as assimilators help students connect new knowledge to existing skills.
Cultures can act as mutual assimilators in a global world.
Large corporations are often assimilators of new technologies.

senses.definition: Causing or related to assimilation, especially of people, cultures, or nutrients.
examples: The state adopted an assimilatory approach to indigenous education.
Assimilatory processes in the body convert food into energy.
The policy had strongly assimilatory goals.

senses.definition: Non-standard or mistaken form of 'assimilate', sometimes used to mean absorb or integrate.
examples: Some speakers mistakenly use 'assimulate' instead of 'assimilate'.
You should write 'assimilate', not 'assimulate', in formal texts.
Learners sometimes confuse and assimulate similar words.

senses.definition: An old or dialect word for a young donkey or a foolish person.
examples: The farmer sold an assinego at the village market.
He called me an assinego for believing the lie.
In the tale, an assinego carries the bags up the hill.

senses.definition: An older spelling of 'Assiniboine', referring to a Native American people of the Northern Plains and their language.
examples: Early records used the name Assiniboin for the group.
The Assiniboin signed several treaties with the U.S. government.
Scholars study the history of the Assiniboin people.

senses.definition: Non-standard or mistaken spelling of 'insinuate', sometimes used to mean suggest something in an indirect way.
examples: He tried to assinuate that I was lying.
Instead of stating it clearly, she seemed to assinuate the point.
Writers should avoid non-standard forms like 'assinuate' in formal texts.

senses.definition: An old or dialect form related to 'assize', formerly used for a court session or legal sitting.
examples: The records mention an assis held in the spring.
At the assis, local disputes were heard and judged.
Only landowners were required to attend the assis.

senses.definition: A medieval legal term, from Latin, referring to a session of a court or a fixed measure set by law.
examples: The charter referred to an assisa regulating bread prices.
Under the assisa, local lords could not overtax traders.
Historians still debate the exact scope of the assisa.

senses.definition: Relating to an assize or court session, in old legal use.
examples: An assisan jury gathered to hear the case.
The assisan records were kept in Latin.
An assisan officer read out the charges.

senses.definition: An old word for 'assize', a session of a court or a regulation fixed by law.
examples: The assise was called to settle land disputes.
An assise on wine prices protected consumers.
The king’s assise limited feudal abuses.

senses.definition: Behaving like a fool or donkey; silly or stupid, especially in an exaggerating way.
examples: It was an assish decision to ignore all the warnings.
He felt assish after making the obvious mistake.
Her assish behaviour embarrassed everyone at the meeting.

senses.definition: In a foolish or silly way.
examples: He grinned assishly when he realized his mistake.
She laughed assishly at her own bad joke.
They behaved assishly during the serious discussion.

senses.definition: Foolish or silly behaviour.
examples: His sheer assishness annoyed his colleagues.
She apologized for her moment of assishness in the meeting.
They laughed at the assishness of their earlier plan.

senses.definition: A town in central Italy, known as the birthplace of St. Francis and a popular religious and tourist site.
examples: They visited Assisi during their trip to Italy.
Assisi is famous for its beautiful basilica and medieval streets.
Pilgrims from around the world travel to Assisi every year.

senses.definition: To help someone to do something.
examples: The nurse will assist you with the form.
Volunteers assist the staff during busy periods.
Could you assist me in finding this address?

senses.definition: In sports, a pass or action that helps another player score.
examples: She had two goals and one assist in the match.
The captain made a perfect assist for the winning goal.
He leads the league in assists this season.

senses.definition: Help given to someone.
examples: Thank you for your assistance with this problem.
They offered financial assistance to the victims.
Please ask a member of staff if you need assistance.

senses.definition: Different forms or instances of help given to people.
examples: The agency coordinates various assistances for low-income families.
Different assistances are available depending on your situation.
The report listed the assistances provided after the disaster.
